title: Dwight Spencer - 0x5DCBF78E3F9C3FE3
description: Systems design, infrastructure sovereignty, and digital civil liberties. Principal, Da Planet Security (est. 2003). Technology Chair, Restore The Fourth.

items.title: Age Verification Is Identity Capture
items.summary: The technical architecture behind age assurance systems — what data is captured, where it flows, who holds it, and why the cryptographic alternative that would solve the stated problem is not being deployed.

items.title: Why Lisp Is Only for Hackers
items.summary: A working session building a Common Lisp AST-to-JSON tool for policy gates and attestation pipelines ended in a structural finding: Common Lisp has no parse phase independent of execution. The archived tool and the governance lesson that follows from it.

items.title: The Awesome Motive Attack Is a CDN Trust Problem, Not a Plugin Problem
items.summary: Malicious JavaScript injected into Awesome Motive's CDN infrastructure backdoored over 1.2 million WordPress sites running OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage. The code was not in the plugins. It was in the vendor-served scripts those plugins load at runtime — a trust boundary most SBOMs do not cover.

items.title: CIRCIA's Town Halls Close This Week. The 72-Hour Clock Doesn't.
items.summary: CISA is taking public input on the CIRCIA final rule through June 18. The 72-hour incident reporting and 24-hour ransomware payment requirements are fixed in statute. What remains under negotiation is scope, definitions, and burden — and that is where the OT and critical infrastructure picture becomes specific.

items.title: The SADM Incident Is an OT Visibility Story
items.summary: Dragos documented an attacker using Claude to pivot from IT to OT at a Mexican water utility. The AI didn't create the exposure. The boundary with no monitoring did. What that means for anyone running operational infrastructure.

items.title: Your SBOM Does Not Know Copilot Wrote That Function
items.summary: SBOM tooling accurately enumerates dependencies. It records nothing about AI-generated code provenance. With CMMC Level 2 Phase 2 assessments starting November 2026, that gap is no longer latent.

items.title: AI Slop and the Missing Audit Trail Are the Same Bug
items.summary: geohot's Eternal Sloptember describes a quality problem. The SBOM AI provenance gap is the same problem from the audit direction. Two sides of one failure mode — and in security contexts, that failure mode is silent until it isn't.

items.title: The Copilot Meter Shock Is a Governance Problem
items.summary: GitHub Copilot switched to token-based billing on June 1. The community is arguing about cost. The actual problem is that AI tooling was never brought under the same governance controls as every other cloud service.

items.title: When the Government Buys the Model: AI, Section 702, and the Surveillance Stack
items.summary: EPIC reported in April that Anthropic's tools are being used to analyze Section 702 data including Americans' communications. The Surveillance Accountability Act would require a warrant before accessing third-party data. Neither story has gotten the attention it deserves.

items.title: Owning Your Memory: Hardened Arenas, Channels, and Structural OOP in Pure C99
items.summary: Reference-counted arena, non-blocking channel, and structural OOP dispatch in ISO C99. Zero external dependencies, zero heap, all ten NASA Power of Ten rules applied. Companion source included.

items.title: Chatrie v. United States: The Cloud Storage Question the Supreme Court Has to Answer
items.summary: The Supreme Court heard arguments in Chatrie v. United States on April 27. The question is whether geofence warrants violate the Fourth Amendment. The real question is whether storing your life on someone else's server destroys your constitutional protection over it.

items.title: Troy, NY: How a Surveillance Contract Captured a City
items.summary: Mayor Mantello declared a public safety emergency to pay a Flock camera contract her city council had blocked. The council filed an Article 78 lawsuit. The fight is still live. The contract mechanism that made it possible — and why it matters beyond Troy.

items.title: neural.sh: A Shell Script Where a Plugin Used to Be
items.summary: After installing ALE and reviewing w0rp's other work, I found neural.vim — well-built, not UNIX. neural.sh is the shell answer to the same problem.

items.title: LLM-Generated Terraform Does Not Know What Your Architecture Is For
items.summary: In May 2023 I ran the same infrastructure build two ways on a live stream — LLM-generated Terraform against cookiecutter atomic modules with OPA/Rego gates. The gap wasn't speed. It was that the model had no concept of what the infrastructure was supposed to accomplish.

items.title: Three Runtimes, One Site: A Case for Self-Documenting Infrastructure
items.summary: On treating a website as a running program — content as queryable facts, build systems as code generators, and output formats as policy. A case for self-documenting infrastructure drawn from systems design principles.

items.title: The Watchers You Fed: Chapter Preview
items.summary: A preview chapter from the forthcoming KDP book on surveillance capitalism, behavioral prediction markets, and the infrastructure of digital control.

items.title: The Shell Is Gone (For Now)
items.summary: hack.dapla.net is offline. What it was, why Soft Serve and Wish never got along, and what the node might become — BBS, CTF, storefront, Gopher, livestream playground.

items.title: Infrastructure Independence: Self-Hosting Without the Hype
items.summary: A practical, no-nonsense guide to self-hosting your critical services. Companion piece to Hacker Public Radio episode — covers what to host, what not to host, and how to avoid the traps.

items.title: Fourth Amendment in the Age of AI Surveillance
items.summary: A technical and legal brief on how AI-powered surveillance infrastructure is outpacing Fourth Amendment doctrine, and what RT4 and technologists can do about it.

items.title: DevOps Before DevOps: The 2007–2008 IBM IP Dispute and What It Means Now
items.summary: A first-person account of presenting Developer Operations concepts in 2007–2008, before the term existed, and the IBM IP dispute that led to the CompuTEK→Da Planet Security rebrand — and what it tells us about corporate appropriation of practitioner knowledge.

items.title: After the Canary: What Happened to the Warrant Canary Standard
items.summary: The Canarytail organization is gone. Its GitHub org was deleted, its website is dark. This is what that means for implementations that depended on it — and what a resilient warrant canary infrastructure actually looks like.

items.title: Rules, Types, and Glue: A Multi-Paradigm Architecture for Game Simulation
items.summary: A technical evaluation of Prolog, ML-style types (Coalton DSL), and Common Lisp as a layered architecture for game simulation engines. Benchmarks across SBCL and ECL, portability findings, and architectural conclusions about where each paradigm earns its place.

items.title: GitHub's TOS Won't Save You: Why Your Code, Privacy & Copyrights Are at Risk
items.summary: How GitHub's Terms of Service — especially Section D.8 — fail to protect your privacy, copyrights, and code from AI training. Includes a full migration guide to SourceHut using gh2srht and public-inbox.

items.title: Privacy Canaries: A Better Tweedy Bird
items.summary: Warrant canaries are statements that declare whether an organization has received government requests for information. Analysis of the standard, its limitations, and implications for privacy.

items.title: 00 Hellowrld
items.summary: Hellowrld: Reality is silly-putty. A reflection on tech, philosophy, and the pursuit of meaningful work in software development.
